title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear a woman called Emily talking about volunteering at a tree nursery. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear a woman called Emily talking about volunteering at a tree nursery. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: A woman called Emily talks about volunteering at a tree nursery.
extracts.audio_script: Hello. I’m Emily, and I coordinate volunteers at Oakridge Tree Nursery, a community project which grows young trees for parks, school grounds and new woodland areas. People often imagine we are based in an ordinary garden centre. In fact, the nursery is on land once used as a [9]tennis club[/9]. The courts had been unused for years, and the flat space was ideal for rows of pots, a greenhouse and covered working areas. We began with a few hundred trees, but we now grow over three thousand each year.||Our work matters because recently planted woodland needs a reliable supply of healthy local trees. We grow oak, birch, rowan and several other species, but the one requested most frequently by schools this season is [10]cherry trees[/10], largely because of their colourful spring flowers. Volunteers do not need any gardening experience. Everyone begins with a tour and training. Some people expect a lesson about tree identification, but the essential first topic is [11]lifting techniques[/11], as trays of pots can be heavier than they look and must be moved safely.||Tasks depend on the season. In the autumn, volunteers gather seeds in nearby woodland; in late winter, they prepare young trees for planting. When beginners join us in spring, their usual first job is [12]filling pots[/12] with the correct mixture of soil and compost. It is straightforward work, but accuracy helps the trees grow well. We provide gardening tools and protective gloves. However, sessions often continue in cool weather, so volunteers should bring a [13]warm hat[/13], even if the forecast does not suggest rain. Our site is quite exposed when the wind comes across the fields.||Water use is a significant concern at the nursery. A small tap is available for emergencies, but we do not want to depend on it. Rain falling onto the greenhouse roof is collected in an underground [14]storage tank[/14], then used through hoses when trees need watering. We are also careful about labelling every tree correctly. Young plants that are ready to move are transported in a [15]trailer[/15] behind our project vehicle, rather than in vans where branches may be damaged. Volunteers may join planting days at schools and parks after they have attended several nursery sessions.||We hold occasional learning events for people who want to develop their knowledge. Last year the first event to fill was about winter pruning, while this year most bookings have been for our session on [16]seed collecting[/16]. Volunteers who complete fifteen hours receive a [17]gardening voucher[/17], donated by a local business, so they can choose plants or tools for themselves. Finally, our nursery needs better access in wet weather. Instead of another plant sale, this summer we are holding a [18]woodland picnic[/18], with activities for families and guided walks. Funds raised will help create safer paths between the growing areas. Application forms are available at the exit.

dialogue.speaker: emily
dialogue.text: Hello. I’m Emily, and I coordinate volunteers at Oakridge Tree Nursery, a community project which grows young trees for parks, school grounds and new woodland areas. People often imagine we are based in an ordinary garden centre. In fact, the nursery is on land once used as a tennis club. The courts had been unused for years, and the flat space was ideal for rows of pots, a greenhouse and covered working areas. We began with a few hundred trees, but we now grow over three thousand each year.||Our work matters because recently planted woodland needs a reliable supply of healthy local trees. We grow oak, birch, rowan and several other species, but the one requested most frequently by schools this season is cherry trees, largely because of their colourful spring flowers. Volunteers do not need any gardening experience. Everyone begins with a tour and training. Some people expect a lesson about tree identification, but the essential first topic is lifting techniques, as trays of pots can be heavier than they look and must be moved safely.||Tasks depend on the season. In the autumn, volunteers gather seeds in nearby woodland; in late winter, they prepare young trees for planting. When beginners join us in spring, their usual first job is filling pots with the correct mixture of soil and compost. It is straightforward work, but accuracy helps the trees grow well. We provide gardening tools and protective gloves. However, sessions often continue in cool weather, so volunteers should bring a warm hat, even if the forecast does not suggest rain. Our site is quite exposed when the wind comes across the fields.||Water use is a significant concern at the nursery. A small tap is available for emergencies, but we do not want to depend on it. Rain falling onto the greenhouse roof is collected in an underground storage tank, then used through hoses when trees need watering. We are also careful about labelling every tree correctly. Young plants that are ready to move are transported in a trailer behind our project vehicle, rather than in vans where branches may be damaged. Volunteers may join planting days at schools and parks after they have attended several nursery sessions.||We hold occasional learning events for people who want to develop their knowledge. Last year the first event to fill was about winter pruning, while this year most bookings have been for our session on seed collecting. Volunteers who complete fifteen hours receive a gardening voucher, donated by a local business, so they can choose plants or tools for themselves. Finally, our nursery needs better access in wet weather. Instead of another plant sale, this summer we are holding a woodland picnic, with activities for families and guided walks. Funds raised will help create safer paths between the growing areas. Application forms are available at the exit.

questions.question: The tree nursery occupies land which used to be a (9) .
questions.explanation: Emily says the nursery is on land that once belonged to a 'tennis club'.

questions.question: The type of tree schools currently request most often is (10) .
questions.explanation: Emily identifies 'cherry trees' as the species most requested by schools.

questions.question: The most important subject in the first training session is (11) .
questions.explanation: Emily says initial training focuses on 'lifting techniques'.

questions.question: In spring, new volunteers normally begin by (12) .
questions.explanation: Emily explains that spring beginners usually start by 'filling pots'.

questions.question: Emily recommends that volunteers take a (13) for cool conditions.
questions.explanation: Emily advises volunteers to bring a 'warm hat'.

questions.question: Rainwater from the greenhouse is collected in a (14) .
questions.explanation: Emily says greenhouse rainwater is stored in a 'storage tank'.

questions.question: Trees ready for planting are transported in a (15) .
questions.explanation: Emily explains that trees are transported in a 'trailer'.

questions.question: The learning session attracting most interest this year is about (16) .
questions.explanation: Emily says the session on 'seed collecting' has most bookings.

questions.question: After fifteen hours, volunteers receive a (17) .
questions.explanation: Emily says volunteers receive a 'gardening voucher'.

questions.question: Summer fundraising will take place at a (18) .
questions.explanation: Emily announces a 'woodland picnic' as the summer fundraiser.
